knowledge.statement: Authentication verifies the identity of a user or system ('who are you?'). Authorisation determines what an authenticated identity is allowed to do ('what can you do?'). They are distinct concerns and should be implemented separately.
SQL injection occurs when untrusted user input is included in a SQL query without sanitisation. The fix is parameterised queries (prepared statements), never string concatenation to build SQL.

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) injects malicious scripts into web pages viewed by other users. Prevent it by escaping all user-supplied content before rendering in HTML, and using Content-Security-Policy headers.

Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF) tricks an authenticated user's browser into making an unintended request to a server. Mitigate with CSRF tokens, SameSite cookie attribute, and verifying the Origin/Referer header.

Passwords must never be stored in plaintext or with reversible encryption. Use a slow, salted hashing algorithm designed for passwords: bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2id. MD5 and SHA-1 are not suitable for password hashing.

Secrets (API keys, database credentials, TLS private keys) must never be committed to source code repositories. Use environment variables, a secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager), or encrypted secrets in CI.
The Principle of Least Privilege grants each user, process, or service only the minimum permissions needed to perform its function. Excess permissions increase the blast radius of a compromise.
Defence in Depth applies multiple independent security controls so that if one layer fails, others still protect the system. No single security control should be the only line of defence.

HTTPS encrypts data in transit between client and server using TLS. All production web applications must use HTTPS for every endpoint. HTTP should redirect to HTTPS, and HSTS should be enabled to enforce this.

TLS 1.2 and 1.3 are the only acceptable TLS versions. TLS 1.0 and 1.1 are deprecated and vulnerable to POODLE and BEAST attacks. Disable legacy TLS versions and weak cipher suites in all server configurations.

HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) instructs browsers to only access a site over HTTPS, even if the user types http://. Set the max-age to at least 31536000 (1 year) and include subdomains for full protection.
Content-Security-Policy (CSP) is an HTTP header that restricts which resources (scripts, styles, images) a browser may load. A strict CSP is the most effective defence against XSS attacks.
Security headers including X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, and Referrer-Policy harden a web application against common browser-based attacks at minimal implementation cost.

JWT (JSON Web Tokens) should be signed with a strong algorithm (RS256 or ES256, not HS256 with a shared secret in multi-party systems). Always validate the alg header; never accept 'none'. Verify signature, expiration, issuer, and audience.

JWTs should have short expiration times (minutes to hours). Long-lived JWTs cannot be invalidated before expiry. Use refresh tokens with secure HttpOnly cookies and rotation to extend sessions safely.

OAuth 2.0 is a delegation framework that allows third-party applications to access resources on behalf of a user without exposing credentials. Use the Authorization Code flow with PKCE for all client-side applications.
OpenID Connect (OIDC) adds an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0, providing a standardised way to obtain user identity information. It returns an ID token (JWT) alongside the OAuth access token.

Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) requires users to provide two or more verification factors. It dramatically reduces the risk of account takeover from stolen passwords. TOTP (time-based one-time passwords) is widely supported and more secure than SMS.

Rate limiting restricts the number of requests a client can make in a time window. It mitigates brute-force attacks, credential stuffing, and denial-of-service. Apply rate limits to authentication endpoints especially.

Account lockout temporarily disables an account after a number of failed login attempts. Implement progressive delays (exponential backoff) rather than hard lockouts to avoid denial-of-service via targeted lockouts.

Input validation must be performed on the server side, even if client-side validation exists. Client-side validation is a UX feature, not a security control. Never trust any data from an HTTP request without validation.

Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) occurs when an application exposes internal object IDs (e.g., /users/123) without verifying the requesting user has permission to access that object. Always authorise, not just authenticate.
Broken Access Control is the top OWASP risk. Enforce access control on every server-side request, not just in the UI. Deny by default and only grant access when explicitly permitted.

Mass assignment vulnerabilities occur when user-supplied data is bound directly to a model object without filtering allowed fields. Always use an allowlist of accepted fields rather than accepting all input properties.

Command injection allows an attacker to execute arbitrary OS commands by injecting shell metacharacters into input that is passed to a system shell. Always use parameterised system calls and avoid shell execution with user input.

Path traversal attacks use sequences like '../' to access files outside the intended directory. Validate and sanitise file paths, use canonical path resolution, and restrict file access to a defined root directory.

Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) allows attackers to make the server issue requests to internal resources that are not accessible from the internet. Validate and restrict URLs that the application fetches on behalf of users.
XML External Entity (XXE) injection exploits XML parsers that process external entity references in user-supplied XML. Disable external entity processing in all XML parsers.

Dependency vulnerabilities are a major attack surface. Use a Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tool (Dependabot, Snyk, OWASP Dependency Check) in CI to detect known vulnerabilities in third-party libraries.
Supply chain attacks target the build and distribution process of software. Use dependency pinning, verify checksums, sign releases, and audit new dependencies before adding them to a project.

Sensitive data must be encrypted at rest using strong encryption (AES-256). Encryption keys must be managed separately from the data they protect. Cloud providers offer key management services (KMS) for this purpose.

Security logging must capture authentication events (success, failure, logout), authorisation failures, and sensitive data access. Logs must be tamper-evident and stored in a separate system from the application.

Secure random number generation must use a cryptographically secure pseudorandom number generator (CSPRNG). Language-standard math.random() or similar is not suitable for generating tokens, nonces, or session IDs.
Session tokens must be random (at least 128 bits), unpredictable, and have a reasonable expiration time. They must be invalidated on logout and rotated after privilege escalation (login, sudo).

Cookies used for session management must have the HttpOnly flag (preventing JavaScript access), the Secure flag (HTTPS only), and the SameSite attribute (preventing CSRF). Use __Host- prefix for maximum security.
Open Redirect vulnerabilities allow attackers to craft URLs that redirect users to malicious sites after visiting a trusted domain. Validate redirect targets against an allowlist of trusted domains.
Security misconfiguration is a top OWASP risk. Disable debug modes, remove default credentials, close unused ports, and apply the principle of least privilege to all cloud and server configurations.

The OWASP Top 10 is the most widely recognised list of critical web application security risks. In 2021 they are: Broken Access Control, Cryptographic Failures, Injection, Insecure Design, Security Misconfiguration, Vulnerable Components, Auth Failures, Integrity Failures, Logging Failures, SSRF.

Threat modelling identifies threats to a system before building it. STRIDE (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) is a framework for categorising threats.

Penetration testing (pentesting) simulates attacks on a system to find exploitable vulnerabilities. It should be performed by qualified professionals and augments automated scanning, which cannot discover all vulnerability classes.
Security by Design means building security controls into a system from the beginning rather than adding them later. Retrofitting security to an insecure design is expensive and often incomplete.
Fail Secure (Fail Closed) means that when a security control fails, it should deny access rather than grant it. A broken authorisation check should block the request, not allow it through.

Certificate pinning ties a server's identity to a specific TLS certificate or public key. It prevents MITM attacks using fraudulently issued certificates but makes certificate rotation complex. Use with caution.
API keys should be treated like passwords: rotated regularly, scoped to minimum required permissions, and revoked immediately if exposed. Never hardcode them in source code or mobile app binaries.
Zero Trust Architecture assumes no implicit trust based on network location. Every request — whether from inside or outside the network — must be authenticated, authorised, and encrypted.

Privilege escalation attacks allow a low-privilege user to gain higher privileges. Prevent by enforcing strict role checks, using separate processes for different privilege levels, and auditing privilege changes.
Clickjacking embeds a page in a hidden iframe to trick users into clicking on invisible elements. Prevent with the X-Frame-Options: DENY header or frame-ancestors in CSP.

Timing attacks exploit differences in response time to infer secret information (e.g., valid usernames, password bytes). Use constant-time comparison functions for all security-sensitive equality checks.

Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) is a formal inventory of all components used in a software product, including versions and licences. It enables rapid response when a vulnerability is disclosed in a dependency.

Responsible Disclosure (Coordinated Vulnerability Disclosure) is the practice of giving a vendor time to fix a vulnerability before publicly disclosing it. Publish a SECURITY.md to tell security researchers how to contact you.

CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) controls which origins can make requests to an API. Never set Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on APIs that use cookies or tokens. Be explicit about allowed origins.
Replay attacks reuse a captured valid request to perform the same action again. Prevent with nonces, timestamps with short validity windows, or HMAC-signed requests that include a request ID.

Cryptographic agility is the ability to replace cryptographic algorithms quickly when they are compromised without redesigning the whole system. Design systems so the algorithm identifier is stored alongside encrypted data.

Container image scanning checks Docker images for known vulnerabilities in OS packages and application dependencies before deployment. Run scans in CI and block deployment of images with critical vulnerabilities.

Static Application Security Testing (SAST) analyses source code or binaries for security vulnerabilities without running the code. It integrates into CI pipelines and catches issues early in the development cycle.

Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST) analyses a running application by sending attack payloads and inspecting responses. It finds vulnerabilities that are only apparent at runtime, such as authentication bypasses.

The Secure Development Lifecycle (SDL) integrates security activities — threat modelling, code review, penetration testing — throughout the development process rather than treating security as a final gate.

Encryption keys must be rotated periodically and whenever there is suspicion of compromise. Key rotation must be designed into the system from the start — retrofitting rotation to a system that was not designed for it is difficult.

Subresource Integrity (SRI) prevents CDN-hosted scripts from being tampered with by including a cryptographic hash of the expected file in the script tag. The browser refuses to execute the script if the hash does not match.

Environment variables are not inherently secure for secrets — they can be leaked via debug endpoints, error messages, and process listings. Prefer dedicated secrets management systems for production secrets.

Log injection occurs when user-supplied input is written to logs and can manipulate log entries to hide malicious activity or inject false records. Sanitise log inputs and use structured logging to prevent it.

Broken Object Level Authorisation (BOLA), also called IDOR, is the most common API vulnerability. Verify that the authenticated user is authorised to access the specific resource identified in every API request.
Security regression tests should be written for every fixed vulnerability. Without a test, the same vulnerability is likely to be reintroduced in a future refactor.
